_source: Gen 2 Compendiums/DATA ANALYSIS - VOL 1 & 2, TASC MTG215123 Module 3 Topic 1. Standard deviation questions use the POPULATION SD (÷n), matching the compendium's stated formula. Answer keys verified by tools/verify_answers.py.

unit.title: Univariate Data Analysis
unit.subtitle: Module 3 · Topic 1
unit.description: The statistical investigation process: classifying data, displaying it, and summarising a single numerical variable with measures of centre (mean, median) and spread (range, IQR, standard deviation), including outliers and comparing groups. Criteria C7 and C1.

lessons.title: Types of data
lessons.overview: Classifying the variable before you analyse it.

sections.title: Categorical vs numerical
sections.html:
Categorical data sorts responses into qualitative categories (no numerical size):

  • Nominal — categories with no order (e.g. mode of transport, place of birth).
  • Ordinal — categories with a natural order (e.g. strongly agree → strongly disagree, small/medium/large).

Numerical data is quantitative — things counted or measured:

  • Discrete — fixed values, usually from counting (e.g. number of siblings).
  • Continuous — any value in a range, usually from measuring (e.g. height, temperature).

lessons.title: Displaying data & describing shape
lessons.overview: Choosing a display and reading its shape.

sections.title: Displays
sections.html: Categorical data suits bar charts and tables. Numerical data suits frequency tables, stem plots, histograms and box plots. A histogram is a bar chart for numerical data where the bars touch.

sections.title: Shape of a distribution
sections.html:
Describe a numerical distribution by its:

  • Modality — one peak (unimodal) or several (multimodal).
  • Shape — symmetric, or skewed (a long tail to the positive or negative side).
  • Centre, spread and outliers — where it sits, how variable it is, and any unusual values.

lessons.title: Measures of centre: mean & median
lessons.overview: Two ways to describe the middle of the data.

sections.title: Mean
sections.html: The mean is the sum of all scores divided by how many there are. For 4, 8, 6, 10, 7: (4+8+6+10+7) ÷ 5 = 35 ÷ 5 = 7.

sections.title: Median
sections.html: The median is the middle score when the data is arranged in order. With an odd count it is the middle value; with an even count it is halfway between the two middle values. For 4, 6, 8, 10 the median is (6 + 8) ÷ 2 = 7.

lessons.title: Measures of spread & outliers
lessons.overview: How variable the data is, and spotting unusual values.

sections.title: Range and interquartile range
sections.html: The range is highest − lowest. The interquartile range (IQR) is the spread of the middle half: IQR = Q₃ − Q₁, where Q₁ and Q₃ are the lower and upper quartiles. The IQR is not affected by extreme values, so it is a robust measure of spread.

sections.title: Standard deviation
sections.html: The standard deviation measures the average distance of scores from the mean — a larger value means more variability. It is calculated with your calculator's statistics summary rather than by hand.

sections.title: Outliers
sections.html: A value is treated as an outlier if it lies below Q₁ − 1.5×IQR or above Q₃ + 1.5×IQR. Box plots make outliers easy to see.

lessons.title: Comparing groups & the statistical investigation process
lessons.overview: Using statistics to answer a real question.

sections.title: Comparing two groups
sections.html: To compare a numerical variable across groups, compare their centres (median or mean) and spreads (IQR, range or standard deviation) — often with parallel box plots — and interpret the differences in context.

sections.title: The statistical investigation process
sections.html: A statistical investigation follows a cycle: pose a question → collect data → analyse (display and summarise) → interpret and communicate the findings. The formal statistical investigation is a teacher-assessed Work Requirement.

quizzes.title: Quiz: Types of data & displays
quizzes.overview: Classifying variables and choosing displays. Unlimited attempts.

questions.prompt: "Mode of transport to college (car, bus, bike, walk)" is what type of data?
questions.explain: The categories have no natural order, so it is categorical nominal.

questions.prompt: "T-shirt size: Small, Medium, Large" is what type of data?
questions.explain: The categories have a natural order (S < M < L), so it is categorical ordinal.

questions.prompt: "The number of siblings a student has" is what type of data?
questions.explain: It is a count of whole things, so numerical discrete.

questions.prompt: "The height of a plant in centimetres" is what type of data?
questions.explain: A measurement that can take any value in a range is numerical continuous.

questions.prompt: Which numerical display is a bar chart whose bars touch, used for grouped numerical data? (one word)
questions.explain: A histogram is a bar chart for numerical data with touching bars.

quizzes.title: Quiz: Centre, spread & outliers
quizzes.overview: Mean, median, range, IQR, standard deviation and outliers. Unlimited attempts.

questions.explain: In order: 2, 3, 5, 7, 9. The middle value is 5.

questions.prompt: Find the lower quartile Q1 of 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13.
questions.explain: Median is 7. The lower half is 1, 3, 5, so Q1 = 3.

questions.prompt: Find the POPULATION standard deviation of 2, 4, 6, 8, 10.
A data set has Q1 = 10 and Q3 = 20. Find the upper outlier boundary, Q3 + 1.5×IQR.
Two data sets have the same mean. Set B has a much larger standard deviation than Set A. This means Set B's data is:

choices: More spread out from the mean

questions.explain: A larger standard deviation means the values are, on average, further from the mean — more spread out.

questions.prompt: The interquartile range equals Q3 minus ______.
questions.explain: IQR = Q3 − Q1 (upper quartile minus lower quartile).
